did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework named DRMv2Net, which integrates DenseNet201, ResNet101, and MobileNetV2 via hybrid transfer learning, to perform multi-class skin cancer diagnosis. The study achieved classification results (96.11% and 96.17% accuracy) on the ISIC and PAD-UFES-20 benchmark datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work introducing a new deep learning framework named DRMv2Net for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis. It details the methodology (hybrid transfer learning using DenseNet201, ResNet101, and MobileNetV2), preprocessing strategies, and reports specific experimental results (accuracy of 96.11% and 96.17%) on benchmark datasets (ISIC and PAD-UFES-20). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICITIIT), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A HYBRID FEATURE LEARNING FRAMEWORK BASED ON DEEP FEATURE INTEGRATION FOR BETTER SKIN CANCER DIAGNOSIS USING TRANSFER LEARNING
journal: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
brief_summary: This study introduces DRMv2Net, a hybrid deep learning framework for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis that integrates DenseNet201, ResNet101, and MobileNetV2 via transfer learning. The model employs advanced preprocessing strategies, including data augmentation, adaptive thresholding for hair artifact removal, and image inpainting, to enhance feature extraction and class balance. Experimental evaluations were conducted on the ISIC and PAD-UFES-20 benchmark datasets to assess classification performance. The proposed framework achieved accuracies of 96.11% and 96.17% on these respective datasets, outperforming standard CNN baselines. These results demonstrate that hybrid feature integration significantly improves classification robustness and reliability for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on multi-class skin cancer diagnosis. The introduction explicitly lists melanoma, basal cell carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas as types of skin cancer. The experiments were conducted on the ISIC and PAD-UFES-20 benchmark datasets, which contain multiple skin lesion classes, but the paper does not list the specific subset of classes used in the classification task or provide per-class metrics.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (discrete diagnostic categories such as melanoma, basal cell carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'multi-class skin cancer diagnosis' and 'skin lesion classification'. The model outputs class probabilities using a SoftMax activation function. Metrics reported include accuracy (96.11% and 96.17%), precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard for classification tasks. The loss function used is cross-entropy (and focal loss), which is specific to classification.

Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with comparisons made qualitatively against baseline architectures without statistical significance testing.

conclusions: The proposed DRMv2Net framework, which integrates DenseNet201, ResNet101, and MobileNetV2 via hybrid transfer learning, achieved classification accuracies of 96.11% on the ISIC 2357 dataset and 96.17% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.
DRMv2Net outperformed baseline CNN architectures (VGGNet, ResNet, MobileNet) in terms of accuracy (0.91), precision (0.90), recall (0.80), and F1-score (0.84).

The combination of hybrid feature integration and advanced preprocessing strategies (data augmentation, adaptive thresholding for hair removal, and image inpainting) significantly improved classification robustness and reliability.

The framework is positioned for deployment in real-world skin cancer screening applications, particularly in resource-constrained clinical and mobile health settings, due to its balance of diagnostic performance and practical scalability.
important_remarks: The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICITIIT 2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may limit the rigor of the validation compared to journal publications.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the specific classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma), making it impossible to assess performance on the clinically critical melanoma class specifically.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the claimed superiority over baseline models (VGGNet, ResNet, MobileNet).
The specific input image dimensions for the proposed DRMv2Net model are not explicitly stated, though 224x224 is implied by the baseline description.
Key hyperparameters for the Focal Loss function (alpha and gamma values) are not specified in the text.
The training schedule, including the number of epochs and batch size, is not reported.
The total number of images and the class distribution for the PAD-UFES-20 dataset are not provided, limiting the assessment of class balance.

The comparative evaluation table (Table 1) does not explicitly state which dataset was used for these specific metrics, creating ambiguity about whether these results correspond to the ISIC or PAD-UFES-20 datasets.
The paper claims potential for deployment in resource-constrained settings but does not provide computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time, model size) to support this claim.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets outside of ISIC and PAD-UFES-20, raising concerns about generalizability to unseen clinical populations.
